data.source: Laboratory experiments with undergraduate student participants
data.sample_size: Study 1a: 132; Study 1b: 206; Study 1c: 139; Study 2a: 117; Study 2b: 72; Study 3: 54; Study 4: 102
data.notes: All studies conducted with undergraduate students at a large public university; stimuli included both fictitious and real brands, persuasive appeals, and magazine advertisements.

method.description: A series of laboratory experiments tested how branding alters the attitude functions associated with products and how this affects the persuasiveness of function-matching vs. mismatching appeals. Participants evaluated products at either the category or brand level, rated perceived benefits and symbolism, and responded to utilitarian or symbolic persuasive messages. Statistical tests (ANOVAs and regressions) assessed interactions between product level, product type, appeal type, and individual attitude functions.
steps: Recruit undergraduate participants for each study.
Randomly assign participants to evaluate products at the category level or the brand level.
Present utilitarian (benefit-focused) or symbolic (value-expression) persuasive appeals depending on condition.
Collect ratings of benefits, symbolism, purchase likelihood, and advertisement evaluations.
Compute difference scores (benefit minus symbolism) to index relative utilitarian vs. symbolic attitude strength.
Conduct factorial ANOVAs (e.g., 2×2 Level × Product Type) and regression analyses to test hypothesised interactions.
Aggregate results across multiple product categories (utilitarian vs. symbolic) and across multiple studies.
method.models: Factorial ANOVA, mixed-model ANOVA, multiple regression
method.outcome_variable: 1. Benefit–symbolism difference scores; 2. Purchase likelihood ratings; 3. Advertisement evaluation ratings
method.independent_variables: Product level (category vs. brand), product type (utilitarian vs. symbolic), appeal type (matching vs. mismatching), individual attitude function scores
method.control_variables: Product order, counterbalancing of question order, brand sets (e.g., Set A vs. Set B), fictitious vs. real brand naming

results.summary: Across studies, branding shifted attitude functions: utilitarian product categories became relatively more symbolic when branded, while symbolic categories became relatively more utilitarian. Consequently, persuasive appeals that matched the category-level function lost their advantage at the brand level. Significant interactions were observed between product level and product type (Study 1) and between level and appeal matching (Studies 2a & 2b). Individual-difference analyses (Study 3) and evaluations of real advertisements (Study 4) supported the generality of these effects.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Interaction Level × Product Type on difference scores (Study 1a)
numerical_results.direction: Interaction indicates branding makes utilitarian categories more symbolic and symbolic categories more utilitarian

numerical_results.outcome_name: Interaction Level × Product Type on difference scores (Study 1b)
Interaction Level × Product Type on difference scores (Study 1c)
Interaction Level × Appeal on purchase likelihood (Study 2a)
numerical_results.direction: Matching appeals superior at category level but not at brand level

numerical_results.outcome_name: Interaction Level × Appeal on purchase likelihood (Study 2b)

metadata.original_paper_title: Branding Alters Attitude Functions and Reduces the Advantage of Function-Matching Persuasive Appeals
